crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects an office document with an embedded VBA project. While this is fairly common it is sometimes used for malicious intent.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

trid.file_type: Word Microsoft Office Open XML Format document (with Macro)
Word Microsoft Office Open XML Format document

attributes.magic: Microsoft Word 2007+

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Remotely Hosted HTA File Executed Via Mshta.EXE
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects execution of the "mshta" utility with an argument containing the "http" keyword, which could indicate that an attacker is executing a remotely hosted malicious hta file
Detects a suspicious process spawning from one of the Microsoft Office suite products (Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Publisher, Visio, etc.)
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Florian Roth (Nextron Systems), Markus Neis, FPT.EagleEye Team, Vadim Khrykov, Cyb3rEng, Michael Haag, Christopher Peacock @securepeacock, @scythe_io

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Potential Suspicious Browser Launch From Document Reader Process
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects when a browser process or browser tab is launched from an application that handles document files such as Adobe, Microsoft Office, etc. And connects to a web application over http(s), this could indicate a possible phishing attempt.
Detects the creation of a new office macro files on the systems

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects an office document with an embedded VBA project. While this is fairly common it is sometimes used for malicious intent.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

trid.file_type: Word Microsoft Office Open XML Format document (with Macro)
Word Microsoft Office Open XML Format document

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The macro code provided contains a subroutine named `AutoOpen()`, which is executed automatically when the document is opened. This behavior is characteristic of macros that may be used for malicious purposes, as they can run without user consent.

Within the `AutoOpen()` subroutine, there is a call to the `Shell` function with a command that executes `mshta`, which is a legitimate Windows utility used to execute HTML applications (HTA files). The command attempts to access a remote URL (`http://192.186.142.74/dll/Mylqn0.hta`). This indicates an attempt to download and potentially execute a file from an external source, which is a common tactic employed by malware to retrieve additional payloads or scripts.

The use of `mshta` is particularly concerning because it allows for the execution of scripts in a way that can bypass certain security measures. Additionally, the URL appears to be obfuscated through the use of an IP address rather than a domain name, which is often a red flag indicating malicious intent.

There are no benign functions or comments present in the macro that would suggest a legitimate purpose. The absence of any error handling or user prompts further supports the conclusion that this macro is designed to operate covertly, without drawing attention to its actions.

Overall, the combination of automatic execution, remote file retrieval, and the use of `mshta` strongly suggests that these macros are intended for malicious activities, such as downloading and executing harmful software on the victim's machine.

The macro code utilizes the "AutoOpen" subroutine, designed to execute automatically upon document opening. This subroutine employs the "Shell" function to launch an external application. The target application is "mshta", a command-line utility for executing Microsoft HTML Applications (.hta files).  The "mshta" command is used to access a remote resource located at "http://192.186.142.74/dll/Mylqn0.hta". This suggests the macro downloads and runs a potentially malicious script from the specified URL.

crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_msg: ET EXPLOIT SUSPICIOUS Possible CVE-2017-0199 IE7/NoCookie/Referer HTA dl
crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_raw: alert http $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET any (msg:"ET EXPLOIT SUSPICIOUS Possible CVE-2017-0199 IE7/NoCookie/Referer HTA dl"; flow:to_server,established; http.uri; content:".hta"; nocase; fast_pattern; pcre:"/\.hta(?:[?&]|$)/i"; http.user_agent; content:"Mozilla/4.0 (compatible|3b 20|MSIE 7.0|3b|"; depth:34; http.header_names; content:!"Referer|0d 0a|"; content:!"Cookie|0d 0a|"; reference:md5,66a42e338e32fb6c02c9d4c56760d89d; classtype:attempted-user; sid:2024449; rev:4; metadata:affected_product Windows_XP_Vista_7_8_10_Server_32_64_Bit, attack_target Client_Endpoint, created_at 2017_07_07, cve CVE_2017_0199, deployment Perimeter, former_category CURRENT_EVENTS, updated_at 2020_11_05;)

crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_msg: ET POLICY Possible HTA Application Download
crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_raw: alert http $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET any (msg:"ET POLICY Possible HTA Application Download"; flow:established,to_server; flowbits:set,ET.HTA.Download; http.method; content:"GET"; http.uri; content:".hta"; nocase; fast_pattern; endswith; http.host; content:!"kaspersky.com"; endswith; reference:url,www.trustedsec.com/july-2015/malicious-htas/; classtype:bad-unknown; sid:2022520; rev:6; metadata:created_at 2016_02_15, updated_at 2020_10_06;)

crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_msg: ET INFO Observed DNS Query to .biz TLD
crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_raw: alert dns $HOME_NET any -> any any (msg:"ET INFO Observed DNS Query to .biz TLD"; dns.query; content:".biz"; nocase; endswith; reference:url,www.spamhaus.org/statistics/tlds/; classtype:bad-unknown; sid:2027863; rev:5; metadata:affected_product Any, attack_target Client_Endpoint, created_at 2019_08_13, deployment Perimeter, former_category INFO, signature_severity Informational, updated_at 2020_09_17;)

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The macros extracted from the document exhibit signs of malicious intent. The macro named "NewMacros.bas" contains a subroutine called AutoOpen(). This subroutine is typically used to execute code automatically when a document is opened, which is a common technique employed by malware authors for persistence and propagation.

The AutoOpen() subroutine in this case uses the Shell function to execute an HTTP request to download a file from a remote server (www.seoulhobi.biz/how/Fmaov0.hta). The use of mshta command indicates that the downloaded file is an HTML application (.hta), which can contain potentially harmful scripts. 

The fact that the macro attempts to download and execute a file from the internet upon opening the document is a strong indicator of malicious behavior. It's likely that the downloaded .hta file contains further malicious code, although without analyzing this file directly, it's impossible to say exactly what it does.

Furthermore, there are no visible attempts at obfuscation or manipulation of macro security settings within the provided code. However, the absence of these techniques does not rule out the possibility of the macros being malicious, as evidenced by the suspicious behavior described above.

In conclusion, based on the analysis of the provided macros, they are rated as malicious due to their attempt to download and execute a potentially harmful file from the internet upon document opening.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects an office document with an embedded VBA project. While this is fairly common it is sometimes used for malicious intent.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

trid.file_type: Word Microsoft Office Open XML Format document (with Macro)
Word Microsoft Office Open XML Format document

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The macros extracted from the document exhibit several signs of malicious intent. 

The macro named "NewMacros.bas" contains a subroutine called AutoOpen(). This subroutine is typically used to execute code automatically when a document is opened, which is a common technique used by malware to initiate its operations without user interaction.

Within this AutoOpen() subroutine, there is a Shell function call that attempts to execute an HTA (HTML Application) file hosted on a remote server with the IP address 192.186.142.74. The HTA file is named "Yluhi0.hta". HTA files are executable and can contain scripts or applications that run in the same context as fully trusted applications on a user's machine. 

The use of mshta command to download and execute an HTA file from a remote server is a common tactic employed by attackers to deliver payloads onto a victim's machine. This method allows the attacker to bypass network security measures and execute potentially harmful code directly from the internet.

In conclusion, the macros extracted from the document show clear signs of malicious intent, primarily through the use of an AutoOpen() subroutine to automatically execute a remotely hosted HTA file upon opening the document. This behavior is indicative of a typical macro virus or malware delivery mechanism.

crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

trid.file_type: Word Microsoft Office Open XML Format document (with Macro)
Word Microsoft Office Open XML Format document

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The macro code provided contains a subroutine named `AutoOpen()`, which is executed automatically when the document is opened. This behavior is characteristic of macros that may be used for malicious purposes, as they can run without user consent.

Within the `AutoOpen()` subroutine, there is a call to the `Shell` function with a command that executes `mshta`, which is a Microsoft HTML Application Host. The command attempts to access a remote URL (`http://192.186.142.74/mn/Xtgnb0.hta`). This indicates an intention to download and execute potentially harmful content from an external source. The use of `mshta` is particularly concerning because it allows for the execution of HTA files, which can contain scripts capable of performing various actions on the system, including downloading additional malware or executing commands.

The presence of a hardcoded IP address in the URL suggests that this macro is designed to connect to a specific server, which is often a tactic employed by malware to retrieve payloads or instructions. Additionally, the lack of any user prompts or warnings before executing this action further supports the conclusion that the macro is intended to operate stealthily, bypassing typical security measures.

There are no benign functions or behaviors present in the macro; all indicators point towards malicious intent, specifically aimed at executing unauthorized actions on the user's machine.

The macro, upon the document opening, executes a command that appears to utilize the Windows Shell. This command calls upon "mshta.exe," a program used for executing HTML Applications (.hta files). The specific command instructs "mshta.exe" to fetch and run a script located at the web address "http://192.186.142.74/mn/Xtgnb0.hta".

attributes.magic: PE32 executable (GUI) Intel 80386, for MS Windows

crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_msg: POLICY-OTHER HTTP request by IPv4 address attempt
crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_raw: alert tcp any any -> any $HTTP_PORTS ( msg:"POLICY-OTHER HTTP request by IPv4 address attempt"; flow:to_server,established; http_header; content:"Host:",fast_pattern,nocase; pcre:"/^Host\x3a\s*(?:(?:25[0-5]|2[0-4][0-9]|[01]?[0-9]?[0-9])\.){3}(?:25[0-5]|2[0-4][0-9]|[01]?[0-9]?[0-9])\s*:?\s*\d*\s*$/ims"; service:http; reference:url,www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec15.html; classtype:policy-violation; sid:50447; rev:1; )

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: The sample exhibits classic malicious dropper behavior. Specifically, function sub_401000 contains an obfuscated payload (a 53-byte array) that is decrypted at runtime using a rolling arithmetic/XOR algorithm. The resulting string is then executed via WinExec with the SW_HIDE (0) parameter to hide its activity from the user. This pattern of runtime string decryption followed by silent command execution is a definitive indicator of a malicious downloader or launcher. Furthermore, the binary utilizes dynamic API resolution and pointer encoding (EncodePointer/DecodePointer) to complicate static analysis.

names: I- Warning- Mail-box has been compromised (Confirm Ownership).eml

attributes.magic: PE32 executable (GUI) Intel 80386, for MS Windows

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Remotely Hosted HTA File Executed Via Mshta.EXE
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects execution of the "mshta" utility with an argument containing the "http" keyword, which could indicate that an attacker is executing a remotely hosted malicious hta file

crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_msg: POLICY-OTHER HTTP request by IPv4 address attempt
crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_raw: alert tcp any any -> any $HTTP_PORTS ( msg:"POLICY-OTHER HTTP request by IPv4 address attempt"; flow:to_server,established; http_header; content:"Host:",fast_pattern,nocase; pcre:"/^Host\x3a\s*(?:(?:25[0-5]|2[0-4][0-9]|[01]?[0-9]?[0-9])\.){3}(?:25[0-5]|2[0-4][0-9]|[01]?[0-9]?[0-9])\s*:?\s*\d*\s*$/ims"; service:http; reference:url,www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec15.html; classtype:policy-violation; sid:50447; rev:1; )

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: The sample is a downloader/dropper that utilizes social engineering to mask malicious activity. The function sub_401000 executes a command via WinExec to download and run a remote HTML Application (HTA) from 'http://192.186.142.74/lib/Szgfj0.hta' using mshta.exe. Simultaneously, it displays a fake 'System Error' message box claiming 'mfc90.dll' is missing to deceive the user. The hardcoded IP-based URL and the use of mshta for arbitrary script execution are clear indicators of malicious intent.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects an office document with an embedded VBA project. While this is fairly common it is sometimes used for malicious intent.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The macros extracted from the document exhibit several signs of malicious intent. 

The macro named "NewMacros.bas" contains a subroutine called AutoOpen(). This subroutine is typically used to execute code automatically when a document is opened, which is a common technique used by malware to initiate its operations without user interaction.

Within this AutoOpen() subroutine, there is a Shell function call that attempts to execute an HTA (HTML Application) file hosted on a remote server with the IP address 192.186.142.74. The HTA file is named "Yluhi0.hta". HTA files are executable and can contain scripts or applications that run in the same context as fully trusted applications on a user's machine. 

The use of mshta command to download and execute an HTA file from a remote server is a common tactic employed by attackers to deliver payloads onto a victim's machine. This method allows the attacker to bypass network security measures and execute potentially harmful code directly from the internet.

In conclusion, the macros extracted from the document show clear signs of malicious intent, primarily through the use of an AutoOpen() subroutine to automatically execute a remotely hosted HTA file upon opening the document. This behavior is indicative of a typical macro virus or malware delivery mechanism.

attributes.magic: Composite Document File V2 Document, Cannot read section info

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects an office document with an embedded VBA project. While this is fairly common it is sometimes used for malicious intent.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
**
The provided macros exhibit clear signs of malicious intent, particularly in the `AutoOpen()` subroutine found in the `NewMacros.bas` file. The presence of the `Shell` function is a significant red flag, as it is used to execute external commands or applications. In this case, the macro attempts to launch an HTA (HTML Application) file from a remote server (`http://192.186.142.74/mn/Xtgnb0.hta`). This behavior indicates an attempt to download and execute potentially harmful content from an external source.

Moreover, the use of `mshta` suggests that the macro is designed to run HTML applications, which can be leveraged for various malicious activities, including executing scripts or downloading additional payloads without user consent. The fact that this action occurs within the `AutoOpen()` subroutine implies that it will automatically trigger when the document is opened, further indicating a self-propagating mechanism typical of malware.

There are no benign functions or comments present that would suggest legitimate usage; instead, the focus on executing code from an external URL strongly aligns with common tactics employed by malware authors to bypass security measures and deliver malicious payloads.

Overall, the combination of executing external commands, the specific use of `mshta`, and the automatic execution upon opening the document leads to the conclusion that these macros are indeed malicious.

The macro contained within `NewMacros.bas` utilizes the `AutoOpen()` subroutine, ensuring that its code executes automatically when the document is opened. The primary function of this macro is to invoke the `Shell` command, which is used to execute external programs. Specifically, it launches `mshta.exe` (Microsoft HTML Application host) and directs it to retrieve and execute a remote HTML Application (.hta) file from the URL `http://192.186.142.74/mn/Xtgnb0.hta`. This method is frequently employed by attackers for initial access and payload delivery, as `mshta.exe` can execute scripts (VBScript or JScript) from remote sources, potentially leading to further compromise of the system, including downloading and executing malicious executables or manipulating system settings. The use of an IP address instead of a domain name for the remote resource can also be a characteristic of malicious activity. The `ThisDocument.cls` module contains standard attributes and no executable code.

attributes.magic: Composite Document File V2 Document, Cannot read section info

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects an office document with an embedded VBA project. While this is fairly common it is sometimes used for malicious intent.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The macro code provided contains a subroutine named `AutoOpen()`, which is executed automatically when the document is opened. This behavior is characteristic of macros that may be used for malicious purposes, as they can run without user consent.

Within the `AutoOpen()` subroutine, there is a call to the `Shell` function with a command that attempts to execute an HTA (HTML Application) file hosted at a specific IP address (`http://192.186.142.74/dll/Mylqn0.hta`). The use of `mshta` indicates that the macro is attempting to launch a potentially harmful application from an external source. This is a significant red flag, as it suggests that the macro is designed to download and execute content from the internet, which could lead to further exploitation or installation of malware on the victim's system.

Additionally, the presence of the `AutoOpen()` subroutine itself is often associated with self-replicating mechanisms in macro viruses, as it allows the code to run automatically upon opening the document. There are no benign functions or comments present that would indicate legitimate usage; instead, the focus is solely on executing external content.

Overall, the combination of automatic execution, external downloads, and the use of potentially harmful commands leads to the conclusion that these macros exhibit clear signs of malicious intent.

The macro `AutoOpen` executes upon opening the document. It uses the `Shell` function to launch `mshta.exe` with a URL as an argument. The URL points to a remote server (`http://192.186.142.74/dll/Mylqn0.hta`) and attempts to download and execute an HTA file. HTA files can execute arbitrary code, which makes downloading and executing one from a remote source a common technique for malware distribution.
